channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Iranians Live With Pain and Powerlessness, Beneath a Smooth Veneer
item.description: After months of upheaval, many are attempting to get on with their lives while quietly grappling with grief, economic stress and a loss of hope.
item.dc:creator: Yeganeh Torbati
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kiana Hayeri for The New York Times
item.media:description: An emotional goodbye on Iran’s border with Turkey before an Iranian family headed to Australia for work, not knowing when they would reunite with loved ones at home.

item.title: Attacks on Jewish Targets in Europe Suggest Hybrid Warfare
item.description: Officials are investigating similar attacks across Europe, all claimed by a shadowy Islamist group that may be using low-cost, unsophisticated methods to sow fear in Jewish communities.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia and Koba Ryckewaert
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: An arson attack in the Golders Green neighborhood of London in March destroyed four ambulances run by a Jewish organization.

item.title: Mother Runs for Office in India After Daughter Was Raped and Killed
item.description: Ratna Debnath is a candidate for Prime Minister Narendra Modi’s party, which is campaigning on women’s safety in an Indian state election.
item.dc:creator: Anupreeta Das and Hari Kumar
item.media:credit: Atul Loke for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ratna Debnath, center, campaigning in the North 24 Parganas district in West Bengal this month.

item.title: Iceland’s Pools and Hot Tubs Are Now UNESCO-Recognized. Some Locals Aren’t Thrilled.
item.description: Iceland’s swimming pools and hot tubs serve as hubs of social life, a cultural distinction recently honored by UNESCO. Some Icelanders aren’t so thrilled.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg
item.category: United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization
item.media:credit: Sergey Ponomarev for The New York Times

item.title: Iran’s Supreme Leader Signals Plan to Maintain Control Over Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei said in a statement that Iran would establish “new legal frameworks” for the Strait of Hormuz. He also said his country would retain its nuclear capabilities.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Iranians held photos of the country’s supreme leader, Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei, at a government-organized march in Tehran on Wednesday.

item.title: What to Know About the Stabbing Attack Against 2 Jewish Men in London
item.description: The British police said the attack on Wednesday was being treated as terrorism, and they warned of rising antisemitic hate crimes.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.media:description: A crime scene investigator at the site of a stabbing attack in the Golders Green neighborhood of London on Wednesday.

item.title: Police in Australia Were Warned of Terror Risk Before Bondi Attack, Report Says
item.description: A Jewish security group told police an attack on the community was “likely” because of heightened antisemitism, days before December’s mass shooting in Sydney.
item.dc:creator: Victoria Kim and Laura Chung
item.media:credit: Matthew Abbott for The New York Times
item.media:description: A memorial at Bondi Beach, the site of the attack, in Sydney, Australia, in December.

item.title: Journalist Detained in Kuwait Says He Was Stripped of Citizenship
item.description: Ahmed Shihab-Eldin, a Kuwaiti-American, was held for several weeks after posting about the Iran war. Kuwait does not appear to have commented publicly on his case.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Nereim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Ahmed Shihab‑Eldin in Doha, Qatar, last year.

item.title: Israel Intercepts Aid Flotilla Heading to Breach Naval Blockade of Gaza
item.description: Activists said the Israeli military boarded and disabled boats carrying humanitarian assistance to the territory in international waters near Greece.
item.dc:creator: Isabel Kershner, Lynsey Chutel and Rawan Sheikh Ahmad
item.media:description: Boats carrying activists and humanitarian aid bound for Gaza, as part of the Global Sumud Flotilla, in Barcelona, Spain, this month.

item.title: Daw Aung San Suu Kyi Is Moved to House Arrest From Prison in Myanmar
item.description: The ousted civilian leader has been detained since a military coup forced her from power in 2021.
item.dc:creator: Pranav Baskar
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
Coups D'Etat and Attempted Coups D'Etat
item.media:description: Protesters with a banner of Daw Aung San Suu Kyi in Yangon, Myanmar, in 2021. Her family has called for her release, warning that her health was deteriorating.

item.title: Ukraine Reacts With Shrug to Phone Call Between Trump and Putin
item.description: More than a year of similar conversations have failed to bring the country any closer to peace, so Ukrainians have stopped hoping they will.
item.dc:creator: Constant Méheut
item.media:description: President Vladimir V. Putin of Russia and President Trump in Anchorage in August. On Wednesday, the two leaders spoke by phone for the first time this year.

item.title: Trump May Pull Troops From Germany. That Isn’t as Scary as It Once Was.
item.description: Germany has hugely increased its military spending, aiming to be less dependent on Washington. Its support for U.S. attacks on Iran may also give it leverage.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley, Christopher F. Schuetze and Steven Erlanger
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: American soldiers in Germany last month. On Wednesday, President Trump declared that the United States was “studying and reviewing the possible reduction of Troops in Germany.”

item.title: How the War in Iran is Affecting Africa’s Economy and Fuel Supply
item.description: Despite the fragile cease-fire in the Middle East, many Africans say they are bracing for tougher times ahead and making difficult decisions about the future.
item.dc:creator: John Eligon, Golden Matonga and Matthew Mpoke Bigg
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A girl using a solar-powered light to study during electricity rationing in Juba, South Sudan, last month.

item.title: In France, Trump’s Triumphal Arch Draws Eye Rolls
item.description: The Arc de Triomphe in Paris, which the president vows to top in Washington, offers a lesson in the complicated history of monuments.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times
item.media:description: Conceived in 1806 by Napoleon, the Arc de Triomphe has come to symbolize very different things in modern France.

item.title: New Banksy Statue Causes Stir in Central London
item.description: The statue depicts a man marching with a flag that covers his face. It appeared in a section of London near statues of 19th-century British military and colonial figures.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.media:description: The artist Banksy appeared to have taken credit for statue of a man holding a flag covering his face that stood in Waterloo Place in London.

item.title: After Seven Years, First U.S. Direct Flight Lands in Caracas
item.description: President Trump banned commercial flights to Venezuela during his first term, but the flights are the latest step in re-establishing ties between the two countries.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager
item.media:description: The first direct flight between the United States and Venezuela landed in Caracas on Thursday afternoon.

item.title: Sheinbaum’s Dilemma in Mexico: Defy the U.S. or Arrest an Ally
item.description: The Trump administration wants President Claudia Sheinbaum to arrest a Mexican governor. She is faced with few good options in response.
item.dc:creator: Jack Nicas and Emiliano Rodríguez Mega
item.media:description: President Claudia Sheinbaum of Mexico could arrest one of her party allies, the governor of Sinaloa state. and extradite him to the United States, or defy the U.S. request and protect him.

item.title: The World Met to Talk Climate Change. The U.S. Wasn’t Invited.
item.description: Dozens of countries met this week to discuss how to end the world’s dependence on fossil fuels, a goal that the Trump administration doesn’t share.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:description: Delegates gathered for a meeting on transitioning away from fossil fuels in Santa Marta, Colombia, on Wednesday.

item.title: How Trump’s Iran Blockade Is Complicating a High-Stakes Trip to China
item.description: If President Trump flies to China as planned in May, the primary topic will clearly be the rippling economic effects of a war that Beijing has made clear it viewed as unnecessary.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger and Tyler Pager
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A mural in Tehran last week showing Iran controlling the Strait of Hormuz. China imports about a third of its oil and gas through the waterway.

item.title: August Wilson Like You’ve Never Heard Him Before: In Italian
item.description: In three U.S. cities, a new production of the playwright’s cabdriver drama “Jitney” will be imported from Italy.
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg and Maurizio Fiorino
item.media:credit: Maurizio Fiorino for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rosanna Sparapano and Miguel Gobbo Diaz, actors in a new revival of “Jitney,” at the final dress rehearsal of the Sardegna Teatro production in Sardinia, Italy.

item.title: Missing Indigenous Girl in Australia Is Found Dead After 5-Day Search
item.description: The police are seeking a man who was last seen holding hands with the 5-year-old girl on Saturday night near Alice Springs.
item.dc:creator: Laura Chung
item.media:credit: Rhett Hammerton/Australian Associated Press, via Reuters
item.media:description: Volunteers and officials searching for a missing five-year-old girl in Alice Springs, Australia, on Tuesday.

item.title: Korean Air Bans Roosters on U.S. Flights to the Philippines
item.description: The airline was used by chicken breeders in Texas to illegally transport roosters for cockfights, an animal welfare group said.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Regalado
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:description: An animal welfare group said that roosters were being shipped from the United States to the Philippines on Korean Air flights.

item.title: Rising Fuel Prices Force Policymakers to Weigh Excruciating Choices
item.description: The Bank of England and European Central Bank held interest rates steady on Thursday, as officials search for signs of possible longer-term damage and warn of the impact of a prolonged energy shock.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Violette Franchi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A gas station in Paris. The war in Iran had an almost immediate impact on European inflation, increasing gasoline prices as well as airfares and other fuel-intensive activities.

item.title: British Royals Crisscross Manhattan in Brief Visit Packed With Photo Ops
item.description: King Charles III and Queen Camilla laid flowers at the Sept. 11 memorial before stopping by an urban farm, the New York Public Library, a business event and a gala.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear, Claire Fahy and Sarah Lyall
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
Camilla, Queen Consort of the United Kingdom
item.media:description: Michael Bloomberg, left, King Charles III and Queen Camilla laid flowers at the 9/11 memorial on Wednesday morning, the first of several stops during the royal couple’s visit to New York City.

item.title: Testing the Waters
item.description: Iran has threatened to start charging a toll for passage through the Strait of Hormuz. Even if it never happens, the idea could unsettle global shipping.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.media:description: Ships off the Singapore coast, near the Malacca Strait, this month.

item.title: Trump and Putin Call for a Brief Cease-Fire in Ukraine
item.description: Previous truces have broken down amid competing accusations of violations, and it is not clear that Ukraine will agree to Moscow’s terms.
item.dc:creator: Valerie Hopkins
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A firefighter worked to extinguish a blaze after a Russian strike in Kyiv, Ukraine, this month.

item.title: U.S. Indictment Accuses Mexican Governor of Conspiring With Sinaloa Cartel
item.description: Prosecutors accused Rubén Rocha Moya, the governor of Sinaloa state, and other Mexican officials of a yearslong conspiracy to protect the powerful cartel.
item.dc:creator: Jack Nicas, Maria Abi-Habib and Emiliano Rodríguez Mega
item.media:description: Rubén Rocha Moya, the governor of Sinaloa state, is the highest-ranking member of Mexico’s dominant political party, Morena, to be indicted by the United States.

item.title: Russia’s Victory Day Parade Will Be Missing Tanks and Other Heavy Military Armor
item.description: The absence of tanks and other heavy military vehicles on Red Square will highlight the heavy toll of the war in Ukraine.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Nechepurenko
item.media:description: Last year’s Victory Day parade in Moscow’s Red Square featured a large force of armored vehicles.

item.title: Almost All of Europe Was Abnormally Hot in 2025, Report Finds
item.description: The continent, which is warming faster than the global average, saw wildfires, floods and a sub-Arctic heat wave, the European State of the Climate report said.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel
item.category: European Center for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts
item.media:credit: Ilvy Njiokiktjien for The New York Times
item.media:description: A scorching day in Seville, Spain, last July.

item.title: Coast Guard Calls Off Search for Crew of Capsized Cargo Ship
item.description: The Mariana, a 145-foot vessel with six crew members, was found overturned after a typhoon, prompting an 11-day search which found no survivors.
item.dc:creator: Isabella Kwai
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: U.S. Coast Guard, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Coast Guard footage of an overturned cargo vessel, the Mariana, in the Pacific. Officials called off the search for its crew members on Tuesday after 11 days.

item.title: Trump’s Clash With Merz Shows It’s Hard to Stay Friends With the President
item.description: Throughout the war in Iran, Friedrich Merz, the German chancellor, did all he could to keep President Trump happy. This week, Mr. Merz appeared to lose patience.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Chancellor Friedrich Merz in Berlin on Monday. He told a group of German students that the Iranian government had “humiliated” America with its slow-walk approach to negotiating an end to the war.

item.title: Indian Tycoon Offers Refuge to Pablo Escobar’s Condemned Hippos
item.description: Colombia was planning to slaughter 80 hippopotamuses after a small herd imported in the 1980s by the drug lord grew out of control. An Indian tycoon has offered them a new home instead.
item.dc:creator: Alex Travelli
item.category: Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species
item.media:description: Hippos descended from four imported by the drug kingpin Pablo Escobar in Colombia, in a 2023 photo.

item.title: Stabbing in Jewish Area in North London Treated as a Terrorist Incident, Police Say
item.description: Two men who were stabbed in Golders Green on Wednesday were in stable condition, the police said. One man was arrested on suspicion of attempted murder.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia and Amelia Nierenberg
item.media:description: The stabbing occurred in the Golders Green neighborhood of London, home to a large Jewish population, and came at a time of heightened anxiety after several antisemitic attacks.

item.title: Men Accused in Fires Targeting Starmer Received Orders in Russian, Prosecutors Say
item.description: Two buildings and a car linked to Prime Minister Keir Starmer of Britain were set afire last year. The men on trial for it were recruited through a Russian-language Telegram account, a jury was told.
item.dc:creator: Lizzie Dearden
item.category: Central Criminal Court of England and Wales (Old Bailey)
item.media:description: A police officer outside a home in north London owned by Prime Minister Keir Starmer after it was damaged by fire last year.

item.title: U.S. Gas Prices Climb Further as Effects of Iran War Reverberate
item.description: Crude oil prices hit a fresh high since the start of the war in Iran, as energy supplies from the Middle East remain disrupted.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: A Look Back at Charles’s U.S. Trips
item.description: From a barbecue at Camp David to a college football game, the British monarch has experienced many American traditions over decades of visiting the United States.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Regalado
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:description: Prince Charles and Camilla, Duchess of Cornwall, toured the Point Reyes farmers market near San Francisco in 2005.

item.title: In France, American Universities Lose Their Allure in the Trump Era
item.description: Worries about visas, academic freedom and safety are making foreign schools, like Sciences Po in Paris, more attractive to some students than the Ivy League.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler and Dmitry Kostyukov
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times

item.title: Loss of Emirates Further Weakens OPEC’s Influence
item.description: The exit of the United Arab Emirates is the most significant in a series of departures from the oil cartel in recent years.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn and Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Oil storage in Big Spring, Texas. OPEC is less powerful than it once was, in no small part because of the rise of U.S. oil production.

item.title: Paraguay Says It’s Not Abandoning Taiwan. China Has Other Plans.
item.description: Beijing is trying to unravel an unlikely long-distance relationship that has endured for decades. Could Paraguay be tempted to stray?
item.dc:creator: Laurence Blair and María Magdalena Arréllaga

item.title: Epstein Obtained Objects From Islam’s Holiest Site for His Island ‘Mosque’
item.description: Jeffrey Epstein’s messages cast light on an unusual building on his private island and show how his connections helped him secure tapestries from Mecca for it.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni

item.title: Meta Charged With Failing to Keep Children Off Instagram and Facebook in Europe
item.description: European Union regulators said the company did not have effective controls to check a user’s self-declared date of birth, in violation of an online safety law.
item.dc:creator: Adam Satariano
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Regulators in Europe said Meta appears to be violating the Digital Services Act, an E.U. law passed in 2022 to force social media companies to police their platforms more aggressively.

item.title: At State Dinner, King Charles Charms the Court of Trump
item.description: King Charles III presented President Trump with a golden bell. “Should you ever need to get hold of us,” the king said, “well, just give us a ring!”
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:description: President Trump and the first lady, Melania Trump, welcomed King Charles III of Britain and Queen Camilla to the White House on Tuesday evening for a state dinner.

item.title: Gallows Humor Provides Respite From Mideast Wars
item.description: Arab digital creators on social media are turning out edgy material to cope with the conflicts and bring levity to a pain that often feels too raw.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Diego Ibarra Sánchez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Samer Moumneh, wearing a red headband, recording a new sketch in a cafe in Beirut, Lebanon, last week.

item.title: Global Deforestation Slows, W.R.I. Report Finds. But Wildfires Are Taking a Toll.
item.description: In 2025, the world razed less forest than any other year in the last decade. The bad news: global warming is making wildfires more frequent and intense.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey and Harry Stevens

item.title: Acid Attack in Indonesia Evokes Brutality of Suharto Era
item.description: The victim had protested against the widening role of the military, which activists say is reminiscent of the country’s decades-long dictatorship.
item.dc:creator: Muktita Suhartono and Richard C. Paddock
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
Kontras (Commission for Missing Persons and Victims of Violence) (Indonesia)
item.media:description: A mural featuring Andrie Yunus, the deputy coordinator of the Kontras rights group, who was the victim of an acid attack, in Jakarta, Indonesia.

item.title: The British public has taken a dim view of the king’s visit.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:description: King Charles III’s visit to Washington has been unpopular among the public in Britain, where some politicians even called for it to be canceled.

item.title: Prime Minister Mark Carney Says Canada’s Economy Is Expected to Grow and Deficit to Fall
item.description: Prime Minister Mark Carney’s budget update keeps to his path of diversifying away from the United States.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:credit: Sean Kilpatrick/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada arriving at Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Tuesday.

item.title: The E.P.A.’s Lost Science
item.description: The agency’s prestigious research office is being dismantled by the Trump administration, a plan to dam the Bering Strait and more climate news.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: Cornell Watson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Bryan Hubbell, the former head of the air, climate and energy program at the Environmental Protection Agency’s research office. “The state of science is struggling,” he said.

item.title: Dirty Gold
item.description: A Times investigation shows how the U.S. and Canadian mints ended up buying gold that came from a Colombian drug cartel.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times

item.title: Britain Summons Iran’s Ambassador Over Message to Iranians in U.K.
item.description: Iran’s embassy in London posted a message on social media inviting Iranians living in Britain to register for a ‘Sacrifice for the Homeland’ campaign.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.media:description: The Iranian ambassador to Britain, Seyed Ali Mousavi, presenting his credentials to King Charles III at Buckingham Palace in June.

item.title: Treasury Issues More Sanctions on Iranian Oil Exports
item.description: The measures aim to crack down on Iran’s shadow banking system and Chinese purchases of Iranian oil.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: “Iran’s shadow banking system serves as a critical financial lifeline for its armed forces, enabling activities that disrupt global trade and fuel violence across the Middle East,” Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said in a statement.

item.title: It’s Not Just War That’s Making the U.A.E. Leave OPEC
item.description: The United Arab Emirates’ decision to leave OPEC has rocked the region, underscoring how the country, at odds with Saudi Arabia, is increasingly charting its own course.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Nereim and Ismaeel Naar
item.category: Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Smoke billowing from an oil facility in Fujairah, United Arab Emirates, in March.

item.title: Mexico Says 4 Foreigners Were at Cartel Raid Where 2 C.I.A. Officers Died
item.description: Mexican officials revealed that four foreigners — not two — were present during a counterdrug operation in northern Mexico where a crash killed two men later identified as C.I.A. officers.
item.dc:creator: Paulina Villegas
item.media:description: A suspected methamphetamine lab in a remote part of the state of Chihuahua, Mexico.

item.title: Prisoner Swap Between Belarus, Poland and Other Nations Frees 10
item.description: Multiple countries took part in the exchange of prisoners, including a well-known journalist released to Poland, as the Trump administration’s outreach to Belarus continued to pay dividends.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Higgins, Nataliya Vasilyeva and Tomas Dapkus
item.media:description: A photograph released by Poland’s government showing Prime Minister Donald Tusk, back to camera, greeting Polish-Belarusian journalist Andrzej Poczobut after his release from a prison in Belarus, in an unknown location somewhere on the Poland-Belarus border on Tuesday.

item.title: Iraq’s Prime Minister-Designate Thrust Into U.S.-Iran Power Struggle
item.description: Ali al-Zaidi, a political unknown, has been tasked with forming a new coalition government. Now, he must grapple with growing U.S. pressure to curb Iranian influence over Iraq.
item.dc:creator: Erika Solomon and Falih Hassan
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The Iraqi prime minister-designate, Ali al-Zaidi, at his office in Baghdad on Tuesday. The photo was released by his office.

item.title: Afghanistan Accuses Pakistan of Artillery Strike on a University
item.description: Pakistan, which has been waging an “open war” on its neighbor, did not acknowledge strikes on civilian areas Monday that officials said killed at least four people and wounded about 70 others.
item.dc:creator: Elian Peltier and Safiullah Padshah
item.media:description: The damaged Faculty of Education building at a university in Kunar Province, Afghanistan, on Monday.

item.title: Sri Lanka Arrests 22 Monks After 240 Pounds of Cannabis Found in Luggage
item.description: The group of Buddhist monks were arrested on Saturday at the country’s main international airport after a trip to Thailand that the authorities said was a cover for narcotics trafficking.
item.dc:creator: Isabella Kwai and Pamodi Waravita
item.media:credit: Ishara S. Kodikara/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Monks arriving at a court after their arrest, in Negombo, Sri Lanka, on Sunday.
